title: Today's Tech Brief: Beans use an immune receptor to call in airstrikes on caterpillars
summary: A look at beans calling in airstrikes on caterpillars and a Pizza Hut AI lawsuit
imageAlt: Image from source article for Beans use an immune receptor to call in airstrikes on caterpillars
imageCaption: Beans use an immune receptor to call in airstrikes on caterpillars
imageSourceTitle: Beans use an immune receptor to call in airstrikes on caterpillars

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your tech brief. Let's dive into some interesting stories making waves in the tech world. First up, scientists have discovered that beans can use an immune receptor to call in airstrikes on caterpillars. Yes, you read that right - beans calling in airstrikes. It turns out that when beans are attacked by caterpillars, they release certain chemical signals that attract parasitic wasps, which then prey on the caterpillars.

According to a report from Ars Technica, this process involves a complex interplay between the bean's immune system and the wasps. It's a remarkable example of how plants have evolved to defend themselves against predators. I mean, who needs a security system when you have wasps on speed dial?

In other news, a frustrated Pizza Hut franchisee has sued the company over a kitchen AI system that allegedly caused $100 million in losses. The system, which was meant to streamline kitchen operations, apparently didn't quite live up to its promise. My read is that this highlights the challenges of implementing AI in complex environments like commercial kitchens.

As I look at these stories, what stands out to me is the creativity and adaptability of nature - and the challenges of technology implementation. From beans calling in wasp airstrikes to AI systems gone wrong, there's always something new to learn and explore in the world of tech.
